coredata.dc:title: Evaluating internalization and recycling of folate receptors in breast cancer cells using quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Monteiro, Camila A.P.
Oliveira, Aline D.P.R.
Silva, Ryan C.
Lima, Rennan R.M.
Souto, Fabricio O.
Baratti, Mariana O.
Carvalho, Hernandes F.
Santos, Beate S.
Cabral Filho, Paulo E.
Fontes, Adriana
coredata.dc:description: Folic acid (FA) regulates metabolic activities essential to the human body. FA receptor (FR) overexpression has been reported for many cancers, but there are still few or conflicting data about FRs in breast cancer cells. Quantum dots (QDs) have arisen as tools to elucidate aspects on FRs, due to their unique physicochemical properties. Herein, QDs conjugated to FA were explored to study the internalization and recycling of FRs in breast cancer cells, using HeLa as an out-group control. QDs were covalently conjugated to FA under different conditions. The best conjugate was applied to study FRs in HeLa, MCF7, MDA-MB231, and T47D cells applying confocal microscopy and flow cytometry analyses. The conjugation efficiency and specificity were evaluated, respectively, using fluorescence correlation spectroscopy (FCS) and saturation assays. FCS confirmed the effectiveness of the conjugation. HeLa and T47D had/internalized a higher amount of FRs (95% and 90% of labeling, respectively) than MDA-MB231 cells (68%). MCF7 cells seem to have very low functional FRs (3%). Saturation assays proved the specificity of QD-FA conjugates and suggested that FR recycling rate is low in the majority of cells studied, except for T47D. QD-FA conjugates were successfully developed. Therapies targeting FRs may be more effective for HeLa, T47D, and MDA-MB231.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Folic acid
Bioconjugation
Semiconductor nanocrystals
Cancer cells
Receptors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Bioconjugation of QDs to Folic Acid

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to the National Council for Scientific and Technological Development (CNPq), the Coordination for the Improvement of Higher Education Personnel (CAPES) and the Foundation for Science and Technology of Pernambuco (FACEPE). This work is also linked to the National Institutes of Photonics (INFo) and Photonics Applied to Cell Biology (INFABIC, co-funded by CNPq , CAPES , and FAPESP (São Paulo Research Foundation).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical characterization of QDs and the QD-FA (1:25) conjugates. (A) absorption (solid line) and emission (dashed line) spectra of QDs. (B) Emission spectra of bare QDs (solid line) and conjugates (dashed line), acquired under λexc = 365 nm. The inset illustrates the visual fluorescence of the QDs-FA conjugates under 365 nm light.

Representative FCS curves of bare QDs (solid line) and QD-FA conjugates (dashed line). The inset shows the mean hydrodynamic diameters (d) for bare QDs and QDs-FA conjugates. Bars represent standard deviation (SD). Mean diffusion times ± SD are also reported on the figure.

Confocal microscopy images of HeLa cells. A and B: cells incubated with QD-FA conjugates before and after the saturation assay, respectively. In green: fluorescence of QD-FA conjugates, in blue: nuclei stained by Hoechst dye. Scale Bar: 25 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Representative flow cytometry histograms for HeLa cells. In (A) labeling by QD-FA conjugates, and (B) cells incubated with QD-FA conjugates after the saturation of receptors with FA. M2 – represents the percentage of labeled cells.

Representative flow cytometry histograms for T47D (A), MDA-MB231 (B), and MCF7 (C) cells. The identification (1) represents QD-FA labeling and (2) cells incubated with QD-FA conjugates after the saturation of receptors with FA. M2 – represents the percentage of labeled cells.

Confocal microscopy images of MDA-MB231. A and B: cells incubated with QD-FA conjugates before and after the saturation assay, respectively. In green: fluorescence of QD-FA conjugates, in blue: nuclei stained by Hoechst dye. Scale Bar: 25 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Average flow cytometry labeling percentages for HeLa, T47D, MDA-MB231, and MCF7 cells incubated with QD-FA conjugates (before and after the saturation assay). Data are represented as average ± standard deviation (SD). All controls were set at 1.0%.

head.ce:title: Evaluating internalization and recycling of folate receptors in breast cancer cells using quantum dots

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Av. Prof. Moraes Rego, S/N, Departamento de Biofísica e Radiobiologia, CB, UFPE, 50670-901 Recife, PE, Brazil.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Folic acid (FA) regulates metabolic activities essential to the human body. FA receptor (FR) overexpression has been reported for many cancers, but there are still few or conflicting data about FRs in breast cancer cells. Quantum dots (QDs) have arisen as tools to elucidate aspects on FRs, due to their unique physicochemical properties. Herein, QDs conjugated to FA were explored to study the internalization and recycling of FRs in breast cancer cells, using HeLa as an out-group control. QDs were covalently conjugated to FA under different conditions. The best conjugate was applied to study FRs in HeLa, MCF7, MDA-MB231, and T47D cells applying confocal microscopy and flow cytometry analyses. The conjugation efficiency and specificity were evaluated, respectively, using fluorescence correlation spectroscopy (FCS) and saturation assays. FCS confirmed the effectiveness of the conjugation. HeLa and T47D had/internalized a higher amount of FRs (95% and 90% of labeling, respectively) than MDA-MB231 cells (68%). MCF7 cells seem to have very low functional FRs (3%). Saturation assays proved the specificity of QD-FA conjugates and suggested that FR recycling rate is low in the majority of cells studied, except for T47D. QD-FA conjugates were successfully developed. Therapies targeting FRs may be more effective for HeLa, T47D, and MDA-MB231.

ce:section.ce:para: Folic acid (FA), also known as pteroyl-L-glutamic acid, is a B complex vitamin (B9) with 0.44 kDa that is not produced by the human organism, and it is acquired through the ingestion of some vegetables, grains, and red meat. FA uptake by cells depends on folate receptors (FRs), which are 38–40 kDa proteins, usually distributed in the cell apical membrane. After FA binds to FR, this complex is internalized in endocytic vesicles. In the cytoplasm, FA is delivered, and FR is recycled back to the membrane [1]. FA is a cofactor for nucleotides biosynthesis, being essential for human body development. A low folate status has been associated with many pathologies, such as neural tube defects, hematopoietic disturbs, cardiovascular diseases, and an increased risk of cancer [2–4]. However, changes in FR expression can also arise in many diseases, and studies have reported the overexpression of this receptor in the human brain, ovary, kidney, and breast cancers [4–9].

Despite the importance, there are various remaining questions about FR and cancer progression. Literature still has few data about this receptor in this disease. Fluorescence based-techniques are attractive tools for bringing to light complementary information, and assisting to elucidate inconclusive aspects related to the cellular dynamics of functional FRs, especially when employed with photostable fluorophores such as quantum dots (QDs). Semiconductor nanocrystals in quantum confinement regime, QDs, are unique fluorescent probes, with high brilliance, resistance to photobleaching, and a versatile/active surface for bioconjugation, with broad applications in biomedicine [10–12]. Studies on FA trafficking performed in living cells with QDs can be valuable for a better understanding of cancer cell biology, which can help to develop as well as to improve site-specific therapeutic approaches that aim this receptor as an entryway for chemotherapy drugs [9,13–15].

QDs and other nanoparticles have been applied to target FRs in cancer cells [16–18]. Nevertheless, comparative studies related to the dynamics of FRs have been little explored. Thus, herein, we developed conjugates based on QDs and FA to evaluate the FR level, internalization, and recycling among cancer cells. Although breast cancer is a leading cause of death in women worldwide and antifolate drugs have been used in chemotherapy [19], there is still a lack of data related to FR cell uptake and recycling. Thus, we quantified and studied the trafficking of FR in T47D (invasive ductal carcinoma), MDA-MB231 (adenocarcinoma), and MCF7 (adenocarcinoma) human breast cancer epithelial cell lines. Additionally, HeLa cells (human epithelial cervical carcinoma) were used as an out-group control, since FR was considerably investigated in this cell line [20–22]. QD-FA conjugation was evaluated by fluorescence correlation spectroscopy (FCS) and the labeling specificity was demonstrated by FR saturation assays. We believe that this comparative study using QD-FA conjugates can assist in the understanding of the biology of cancer cells by providing information about functional FRs, which can contribute to improving the diagnosis and therapy of this disease.

Aqueous colloidal CdTe QDs were prepared following a methodology previously described with minor modifications [12,23]. Briefly, metallic tellurium powder (Te0, 0.1 mmol – Sigma-Aldrich) was reduced to Te2− with sodium borohydride (NaBH4–3 mmol – Sigma-Aldrich) at high pH (> 10, using NaOH 2 M – 100 μL), and in an inert nitrogen atmosphere (N2). Then, the solution containing Te2− ions was added to a Cd2+/MSA solution, prepared with CdCl2 (0.5 mmol – Sigma-Aldrich) and mercaptosuccinic acid (MSA – 0.6 mmol – Sigma-Aldrich) as the stabilizing/functionalizing agent, also at high pH, in a molar ratio of 5:1:6 (Cd:Te:MSA). The reaction remained under stirring and heating at 90 °C for 40 min to allowed the growth of nanoparticles until a green emission has been reached.

QDs were optically characterized by absorption (UV–Vis 1800 – Shimadzu) and emission spectroscopies (LS 55 – PerkinElmer, at 365 nm excitation). QDs average diameter was estimated by using the wavelength corresponding to the first maximum absorption peak [24]. QD concentration was determined using the CdTe QD extinction coefficient [25] and the Beer-Lambert law.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioconjugation of QDs to Folic Acid
ce:section.ce:para: QDs were conjugated to FA (Sigma-Aldrich) using carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC – Fluka) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS – Sigma-Aldrich) as coupling agents [26]. To reach a successful conjugation some previous tests were performed considering, not only QD:FA ratios from 1:5, 1:25, and 1:50, but also two variations of NHS (5 and 10 mM), keeping EDC at 2 mM. To choose the most promising conjugation, physicochemical parameters of conjugate suspensions, such as fluorescence maintenance and colloidal stability, were evaluated. Based on these criteria, the system with 1:50 was excluded. The remaining systems were then further evaluated, and selected, according to the labeling performance obtained by fluorescence microscopy.

For conjugation, the pH of the QDs suspension was adjusted to 5.5, with MSA (4.9% w/v), and purified by centrifugation at 1109 × g (Hettich Zentrifugen - Universal 320 R) for 3 min, using a 10-kDa membrane ultrafiltration column (GE Healthcare). The suspension of QDs was purified to (i) remove any excess of MSA and avoid unspecific bounds between free MSA and FA, and (ii) to withdraw the excess of reagents used in the synthesis of QDs. Carboxyl groups of QDs (500 μL of the suspension) were activated with EDC and NHS (250 μL each), according to the molar concentrations described above. Finally, FA was added and samples were kept under shaking overnight at room temperature (~ 25 °C). Conjugates were also characterized by absorption and emission spectroscopies, as above described.
Before the cell labeling, activated carboxyl groups of conjugates (1 mL) were blocked with 50 μL TRIS base (0.1 mol·L−1) for 2 h under shaking to avoid non-specific labeling.

FCS analysis was performed for bare QDs and for the best conjugate in a confocal microscopy (LSM 780 – Carl Zeiss) using a 40× water immersion objective (NA = 1.0). Samples were excited by a blue laser (λ = 488 nm – 1 μW). Under these conditions, 10 correlation curves were acquired and from these curves, diffusion times (τD) were obtained for each sample. The hydrodynamic radius (R) was determined using Eq. (1):
(1)
R =
4
k B
T
τ D
6 πη
ω x 2
where kB is Boltzmann Constant, T is temperature of the sample under laser irradiation (T ~ 303 K), τD is diffusion time, η is medium viscosity (for water η = 7.98 × 10−4 Pa.s) and ωx is lateral radius of the focal volume (230 nm for 488 nm excitation used here). In an FCS analysis, it is possible to confirm conjugation by comparing the hydrodynamic radius and diffusion times of bare QDs and conjugates since higher values are expected for these parameters when an effective conjugation is obtained [12].

HeLa cells were obtained from American Type Culture Collection. MCF7, MDA-MB231 and T47D cells were acquired from Rio de Janeiro Cell Bank. HeLa, MCF7, MDA-MB231, and T47D cells were grown, in DMEM (Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium – Sigma-Aldrich) or RPMI-1640 media (Gibco). All media were supplemented with 10% of fetal bovine serum (FBS – Gibco), 100 mg·mL−1 streptomycin, and 100 units·mL−1 penicillin (Sigma-Aldrich). Cells were grown at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2. When 90% confluence was reached, cells were detached from flasks with trypsin-EDTA 0.25% (Gibco). Cells were then seeded in a 6-well plate (Greiner Bio-One) for flow cytometry evaluation (2 × 105 cells/well) and in 4-well culture dish with glass bottom (Greiner Bio-One) for confocal microscopy analysis (5 × 104 cells/well), and then incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in a humified atmosphere with 5% CO2.

After incubation for 24 h, cells were washed with phosphate buffered saline 1× (PBS - Gibco) and the following conditions were assayed: 1 – control cells, and 2 – cells incubated with conjugates (ca. 3 μmol·L−1), for 30 min.

For confocal analyses, cells were washed with PBS and incubated with the fluorescent Hoechst dye (Sigma-Aldrich, at final concentration of 0.6 μg·mL−1) for 5 min and then washed again with PBS twice before being analyzed under a multispectral confocal microscopy (LSM 780 – Carl Zeiss – with 40×/NA =1.0 water and 63×/1.4 oil immersion objectives). For conjugates, the emission was collected from 540 to 600 nm, at 488 nm excitation. For Hoechst dye, the emission was collected from 420 to 470 nm, with excitation at 405 nm.

The level, internalization and recycling rate of FA receptors were evaluated by flow cytometry (BD Accuri™ C6, Becton Dickinson). For this, cells were detached from the 6-well plate upon incubation with trypsin-EDTA followed by inactivation with the appropriate culture medium. Then, cell suspensions were washed twice by centrifugation with PBS at 400 × g for 2 min (MiniSpin – Eppendorf). A total of 20,000 events were acquired for each experimental condition, under excitation at 488 nm. The emission was detected with the FL2 filter (585/40 nm) and the data were processed using the BD Accuri C6 software.

To confirm the conjugate specificity and to study the FA receptor recycling, saturation assays were also performed. For this, cells were previously incubated with an excess of FA (30× higher than the amount used for conjugation), for 20 min. Then, cells were incubated with conjugates for further 30 min before flow cytometry or confocal microscopy analyses, by using the parameters detailed above. All experiments involving cells were performed at least in triplicate.

According to optical characterizations, QDs had the first absorption maximum at ca. 512 nm, as observed in Fig. 1A. The nanocrystals average diameter and suspension concentration were estimated as 2.8 nm and 10 μmol·L−1 [19,20]. QDs showed a maximum emission at 557 nm (Fig. 1A) and a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of about 53 nm.

In preliminary tests with HeLa cells, we observed differences in the labeling pattern and efficiency, which were related to the different variations of QD:FA ratio and NHS amount used. The system with 1:25 (QD:FA) and the highest amount of NHS was chosen since it led to the highest fluorescence intensity and a characteristic vesicular labeling-pattern, when compared to the low signal obtained with 1:5 conjugates as well as with the smallest NHS amount. Probably, in the system with 1:5 (QD:FA) there were too few folic acid molecules, and the smallest quantity of NHS applied was not sufficient to promote an efficient conjugation.

Moreover, no considerable change in the absorption spectrum profile of the chosen conjugate (1:25) was observed, as compared to the bare QDs. Additionally, this conjugate showed great stability (no precipitation) and bright fluorescence, under UV 365 nm visualization as well as by emission spectroscopy characterization (Fig. 1B). After conjugation, there was a decrease only of ca. 17% in the fluorescence intensity and there were no noteworthy changes in the FWHM. Besides, a discreet redshift was also noted, which can be assigned as indicative of conjugation, as already suggested in other studies [12,27].

QD-FA conjugates showed higher diffusion time and diameter when compared to bare CdTe nanocrystals. This result confirms that QDs were successfully conjugated with FA. FCS data for bare QDs and QD-FA conjugates are shown in Fig. 2 . The diffusion time and hydrodynamic diameter obtained from FCS were already used to verify the conjugation of QDs with larger molecules, such as anti-A antibody and UEA I lectin [12]. The size of the bare QD obtained for FCS analysis (2.6 nm) corroborated with the diameter obtained by the absorption spectra (ca. 2.8 nm). The conjugate hydrodynamic diameter and diffusion time had, respectively, a change of about 0.9 nm and 20 μs when compared to the bare QD. These changes seem to be in accordance with the FA molecular weight of only 0.44 kDa or about 10 kDa when considering that all FA was on the QD surface.

HeLa cells were chosen to evaluate the specificity and efficiency of the QD-FA conjugate, because this cell line is one of the most studied, and according to literature, presents a high FR expression [20,21]. The microscope images showed small vesicles into the HeLa cells cytoplasm (Fig. 3A), suggesting specific labeling with the QD-FA conjugates. The same vesicular labeling pattern observed by us was described by Wang & Low [20] and Lei et al. [21] who also studied FR in HeLa cells. Our results also showed that FA internalization was fast, and 30 min of incubation was enough for receptor endocytosis. The use of Hoechst nuclear dye also allowed observing the intracellular localization of internalized receptors, revealing a perinuclear vesicular pattern (in green) in contrast with nuclei stained in blue.

Flow cytometry analysis was also carried out and indicated that about 95% of HeLa cells expressed and internalized FR receptors, as shown in a representative histogram depictured in Fig. 4A and Table 1 . Zhang et al. [22] developed a probe conjugating by adsorption CdS/ZnS QDs coated with polyethylenimine and FA, which led to a decrease in the QD fluorescence. However, when FR was expressed on the membrane, FA bound to them and the QD fluorescence aroused. The authors found a percentage of FR labeling of about 86% in HeLa cells, corroborating our cytometric results.

After previous saturation of FRs, the labeling was only about 6% (Fig. 4B and Table 1), proving the specificity of conjugates. HeLa cells showed to be a suitable choice as a model to standardize QD-FA conjugate preparation. Indeed, our conjugates remained functional at least 3 months. Micrographs corroborate the flow cytometry results showing noteworthy labeling after the saturation (Fig. 3B).
After proving the QD-FA conjugate effectiveness and specificity, it was carried out studies related to the FR expression and internalization in MDA-MB231, MCF7, and T47D breast cancer cell lines.

T47D labeled about 90%, MDA-MB231 about 68%, and MCF7 did not present relevant labeling (3%) under the conditions studied, as shown in Table 1. Thus, MDA-MB231 cells expressed and internalized FRs, but at a lower rate when compared to HeLa and T74D. Fig. 5 shows representative flow cytometry histograms for MCF7, MDA-MB231, and T47D cells.

Previous studies have investigated FRs in tumors, including breast, lung, colorectal, and bladder cancers [4–9]. Hartman et al. [28] studied samples from patients with invasive breast cancer and showed that there was a correlation between the FR expression and the tumor grade. Moreover, Ginter et al. [29] studied triple negative invasive cancer and observed higher levels of FR in worse outcomes. Aboulhagag et al. [30] studied the FR expression in tissue blocks of invasive ductal carcinoma immunohistochemically stained with FR-antibody, showing overexpression of this receptor and also associating with worse outcome. In our study, MDA-MB231, a more aggressive cell line and classified as triple negative, was labeled about 68% with the QD-FA conjugate. This result suggests that this cell line expressed and internalized FRs, but in a minor rate than HeLa (95%) and T47D cells (90% - considered less aggressive). Inhibition, or reduction, of the labeling after saturation assays for MDA-MB231 (4%) and T47D (26%) showed that QD-FA conjugates also specifically recognized FRs in those cells (Table 1). Moreover, the receptor-recycling rate seems to be low for MDA-MB231 and HeLa cells, unlike T47D cells that demonstrate a more active FR metabolism when compared to the other lineages, under the experimental conditions applied.

Additionally, confocal microscopy images, as represented in Fig. 6 for MDA-MB231, also presented a typical vesicular labeling pattern when labeled by QD-FA conjugates. No noteworthy fluorescence was observed after saturation with excess of FA.

Jain el al. [31] incubated T47D and MDA-MB231 cells with fluorescent rare earth-doped nanoparticles associated with FA. They observed that both cells were labeled by confocal microscopy, reporting higher fluorescence to T47D cells, corroborating with our cytometric results.

Additionally, the literature has also reported conflicting studies about FRs and MCF7, a cell line less aggressive than MDA-MB231. Sonvico et al. [32] used FA conjugated with fluorescent superparamagnetic nanoparticles to label MCF7 and observed that these cells did not have FRs on their membrane. Feng et al. [33] also studied MCF7 FRs using graphene oxide (GO) conjugated to FA and rhodamine B (RB) as a probe with the capability of an on/off response. When FA bound to FR, there was fluorescence transfer from GO to RB. A fluorescence signal was observed for HeLa cells, but not to MCF7. Otherwise, Wang et al. [34] developed a folate-PEG-cationic modified chitosan liposome and reported a considerable folate uptake by MCF7 cells. Tavassolian et al. [9] delivered poly (L-γ-glutamyl-glutamine) nanoparticles with docetaxel and coumarin to MCF7 cells and indicated a higher FR microscopy labeling, after a long incubation time (4 h). Our results suggested that MCF7 cells seem to have very low functional FA receptors, considering the time studied (30 min), compared with MDA-MB231, T47D, and HeLa cells.

Various studies about FRs have been reported, nevertheless, it seems that there has been no consensus about this receptor for MCF7 cells [9,32–34]. Thus, complementary ways to study this receptor could help to elucidate these aspects still conflicting. For our best knowledge, this is the first study that developed QD-FA conjugates, proved their specificity through saturation assays and applied them to study the FR internalization and recycling in breast cancer cell lines (MCF7, MDA-MB231, T47D), and HeLa cells. Moreover, this study also evaluated and correlated the functional FR level, internalization, and recycling, among the cells studied, by using two complementary fluorescence-based techniques (flow cytometry and fluorescence microscopy).

FRs may be an important target for cancer with fewer therapeutic options, as shown by Iannazzo et al. [35] who applied QDs conjugated to doxorubicin and biotin. They used the increased expression of biotin receptors to successfully deliver the drug to human lung adenocarcinoma (A549) cells, aiming a better cancer therapeutic approach. Therefore, a better understanding of functional FRs dynamics can help to develop new approaches to treat cancer, mainly for those with fewer therapeutic options available, like triple negative or not responsive carcinoma.

In this study we developed a simple and effective strategy to conjugate FA with CdTe QDs. Results showed that HeLa and T47D had and internalized a higher amount of FRs when compared to MDA-MB231 cells. However, MCF7 cells seem to have very low functional FRs. Saturation assays evidenced QD-FA labeling specificity and suggested that FR recycling rate may be low for HeLa and MDA-MB231 cell lines, while for T47D was about 26%.

This study suggested that therapy involving FR routes could be more effective mainly for T47D (high FR uptake/expression and recycling rate), but may be also applied for HeLa, and MDA-MB231 (because FR internalization/level), but not for MCF7 due to lack of functional FRs observed. Thus, we believe that this study can help to complement the understanding of cancer cell biology by providing information on the uptake/level and recycling of functional FRs.

CAPM participated in all experiments involved in the study; ADPRO, RCS and RRML synthesized and characterized the quantum dots and conjugates; BSS analyzed and discussed the bioconjugation; FOS, MOB, HFC, PECF and AF analyzed and interpreted the cell labeling; AF and PECF conceived and designed the experiments. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the National Council for Scientific and Technological Development (CNPq), the Coordination for the Improvement of Higher Education Personnel (CAPES) and the Foundation for Science and Technology of Pernambuco (FACEPE). This work is also linked to the National Institutes of Photonics (INFo) and Photonics Applied to Cell Biology (INFABIC, co-funded by CNPq, CAPES, and FAPESP (São Paulo Research Foundation).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structural basis for molecular recognition of folic acid by folate receptors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Chen, J. Ke, X. Edward Zhou, W. Yi, J.S. Brunzelle, J. Li, E.L. Yong, H.E. Xu, K. Melcher, Structural basis for molecular recognition of folic acid by folate receptors, Nature. 500 (2013) 486–489. doi:10.1038/nature12327.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Prevention of neural tube defects: results of the Medical Research Council vitamin study

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Sneddon, J. Densem, C. Frost, R.S. For, N. Wald, Prevention of neural tube defects: Results of the Medical Research Council Vitamin Study, Lancet. 338 (1991) 131–137. doi:10.1016/0140-6736(91)90133-A.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Prevention of the first occurrence of neural-tube defects by periconceptional vitamin supplementation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.E. Czeizel, I. Dudas., Prevention of the First Occurrence of Neural-tube Defects by Periconceptional Vitamin Supplementation, N. Engl. J. Med. 327 (1992) 1832–1835. doi:10.1056/NEJM199212243272602.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Role of the folate receptor in ovarian cancer treatment: evidence, mechanism, and clinical implications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I.B. Vergote, C. Marth, R.L. Coleman, Role of the folate receptor in ovarian cancer treatment: evidence, mechanism, and clinical implications, Cancer Metastasis Rev. 34 (2015) 41–52. doi:10.1007/s10555-014-9539-8.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Expression and differential polarization of the reduced-folate transporter-1 and the folate receptor α in mammalian retinal pigment epithelium

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.D. Chancy, R. Kekuda, W. Huang, P.D. Prasad, J.M. Kuhnel, F.M. Sirotnak, P. Roon, V. Ganapathy, S.B. Smith, Expression and differential polarization of the reduced-folate transporter-1 and the folate receptor α in mammalian retinal pigment epithelium, J. Biol. Chem. 275 (2000) 20676–20684. doi:10.1074/jbc.M002328200.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The folate receptor in central nervous system malignancies of childhood

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Folate receptor α expression level correlates with histologic grade in lung adenocarcinoma
